Post Theme: Kickoff: why I'm building this in public
Hook / Opening Line Idea: I'm building a full wedding-planning SaaS for the Ghanaian market with an AI assistant baked in — and I'm posting every day of it. Here's the plan.
Key Points to Include: What AkomaPlanner is (wedding planner + BisaAI assistant, Ghana market); why you chose this project; that you're using Claude Code and doing 80% AI-assisted / 20% hands-on polish yourself; what people can expect from the series (real decisions, real bugs, not just a highlight reel)
Suggested Visual: Screenshot of the landing page
Notes: Set expectations that this is an honest build log, not a polished announcement

Post Theme: The architecture decision nobody sees
Hook / Opening Line Idea: Before writing a single UI component, I had to answer: where does business logic live, and why does that matter?
Key Points to Include: Full-stack Next.js vs separate backend vs tRPC — why a split backend was overkill here; the specific seam that makes 'swap SQLite for Postgres' or 'swap mock AI for real AI' a config change instead of a rewrite
Suggested Visual: Simple diagram: Browser -> Server Component/Action -> lib/ -> Prisma -> DB
Notes: Good 'here's how I think about architecture' post for recruiters/engineers

Post Theme: The money question every app gets wrong
Hook / Opening Line Idea: Should you store money as a decimal or as an integer? I made a deliberate tradeoff here — and I know exactly when I'd be wrong to make it.
Key Points to Include: Float vs integer minor units (pesewas) for storing GHS; why Float was fine here (planning estimates, not a payments ledger) vs when it becomes non-negotiable (real transactions)
Suggested Visual: Code snippet of the schema comment explaining the tradeoff
Notes: Classic interview-question topic — good engagement bait for devs

Post Theme: I made the AI assistant structurally incapable of overpromising
Hook / Opening Line Idea: BisaAI can draft a message to a vendor. It can never send one. Here's how I made that a guarantee, not a promise.
Key Points to Include: The DRAFT -> SENT -> QUOTED -> ACCEPTED state machine; why lib/bisaai.ts can only write to an audit-log table and nothing else; 'the safety property is enforced by which code is allowed to call which function, not just what the UI shows'
Suggested Visual: Diagram of the state machine with arrows
Notes: This is your strongest 'AI safety in practice' post — lead candidate for most shares

Post Theme: A bug that only showed up once I actually tested it
Hook / Opening Line Idea: My checklist feature passed every check... until I added a task without a due date and it jumped to the top of the list, ahead of things that were overdue.
Key Points to Include: SQL sorts NULL first by default in ascending order; how seeded 'always has data' demos hide this exact class of bug; the one-line Prisma fix (nulls: 'last')
Suggested Visual: Before/after screenshot of the checklist ordering

Post Theme: Testing floating point math taught me something about my own code
Hook / Opening Line Idea: A test that should have trivially passed instead told me 55 does not equal 55.
Key Points to Include: 5500/10000*100 = 55.00000000000001 in JavaScript; why toBeCloseTo() exists; tying it back to the earlier float-vs-integer money decision
Suggested Visual: Code snippet of the failing assertion

Post Theme: The most dangerous bug in this whole project (and dev mode hid it completely)
Hook / Opening Line Idea: Every page passed every test. Then I ran the actual production build for the first time — and found out my dashboard would have frozen in time for every user, forever.
Key Points to Include: Static vs dynamic rendering in Next.js; why `next dev` never reveals this; what 'the page is prerendered once at build time' actually means for a live app; the one-line fix
Suggested Visual: Screenshot of the next build route table (○ Static vs f Dynamic)
Notes: Your single best 'lessons for other devs' post — very shareable, genuinely useful

Post Theme: I built a paywall and then refused to finish it as written
Hook / Opening Line Idea: The spec told me exactly what to put on the pricing page. I left two features off it anyway.
Key Points to Include: 'Full cloud backup/restore' and 'unlimited AI image generation' weren't real features in the app; why listing them on a paywall would be dishonest; the principle of not overstating capabilities, especially on the page tied to payment
Suggested Visual: Screenshot of the final pricing page feature list
Notes: Good values/integrity post — shows judgment, not just execution

Post Theme: Consent isn't a checkbox, it's an architecture decision
Hook / Opening Line Idea: The dress try-on feature never even has the ability to receive your photo on the server. Here's why that's a stronger guarantee than a privacy policy.
Key Points to Include: URL.createObjectURL for in-browser-only photo preview; the server action literally has no photo parameter; UI-level disabled button vs the real enforcement one layer down
Suggested Visual: Screenshot of the consent flow

Post Theme: Why I made my 'AI' honest about not being AI
Hook / Opening Line Idea: Every mock response in this app is labelled as a mock. That was harder to stick to than it sounds.
Key Points to Include: The MockBadge / EstimateBadge pattern; why some 'mocks' are actually just honest rule-based logic (timeline generator, seating suggestions) vs true placeholders (chat Q&A); the discipline of asking 'is this really a stand-in, or is it just done?'
Suggested Visual: Screenshot of a BisaAI tool card showing the Mock badge

Post Theme: The config file that was quietly broken since day one
Hook / Opening Line Idea: My linter had been silently doing nothing for the entire build. I only found out when I finally ran it on purpose.
Key Points to Include: ESLint 9's flat-config migration; how eslint.config.js never got created during scaffolding; the 7 real (small) issues it found the moment it actually worked; 'a tool that silently no-ops is worse than one that's obviously missing'
Suggested Visual: Terminal screenshot of the lint output

Post Theme: My .gitignore did nothing for the entire project
Hook / Opening Line Idea: node_modules and .next were about to get committed to git. The file that was supposed to stop that had been broken the whole time.
Key Points to Include: UTF-16 vs UTF-8 encoded text files; how `git status` showing folders that 'should' be invisible is the tell; git check-ignore -v as the verification step
Suggested Visual: Terminal screenshot showing the fix
Notes: Pairs well as a 'boring bugs matter too' post

Post Theme: When 'it works on my machine' almost got me
Hook / Opening Line Idea: Right after I called this MVP done, the whole site suddenly looked unstyled. The cause: two commands that should never run against the same folder at once.
Key Points to Include: next dev vs next build/start sharing the .next directory; why running a production build test while the dev server was still up corrupted the CSS pipeline; the fix and the actual lesson (separate distDir, or never run them concurrently)
Suggested Visual: Before/after screenshot of the broken vs fixed dashboard styling

Post Theme: Proving an AI safety rule is real, not just documented
Hook / Opening Line Idea: Anyone can write 'the AI can never book anything on its own' in a comment. I wanted to prove it.
Key Points to Include: Flipping a test wedding to Pro, running the full vendor negotiation lifecycle end to end through the real UI, then checking the raw database record instead of trusting the screen
Suggested Visual: Screenshot of the 'Booked' confirmation + the DB query result side by side

Post Theme: A privacy decision nobody asked me to make
Hook / Opening Line Idea: I built a public, no-login link anyone can share for their wedding. Then I deliberately made it show less than it technically could.
Key Points to Include: The share page has full database access but only renders names/date/countdown/aggregate stats — no budget, no guest list, no vendor quotes; 'the least private plausible reading of a sharing feature is usually the right default'
Suggested Visual: Screenshot of the public share page

Post Theme: Where I drew the line on what to build vs what to say I built
Hook / Opening Line Idea: The spec asked for accommodation recommendations 'by side' (bride's family vs groom's). I didn't build that. Here's why, and what I did instead.
Key Points to Include: Schema was designed as global reference data with no per-wedding relationship; adding a new table mid-build for one feature is scope creep worth flagging, not quietly doing; the honest workaround
Suggested Visual: N/A

Post Theme: Milestone: the MVP is feature-complete
Hook / Opening Line Idea: Every feature from my original plan is built, and — more importantly — every one of them has been clicked through in a real browser, not just typechecked.
Key Points to Include: Recap of the full feature list; the Playwright-driven verification habit throughout the build (screenshot every meaningful interaction, not just trust the code); what 'done' actually meant here
Suggested Visual: Full-page screenshot of the dashboard
Notes: Good weekly-recap-style post if you're batching

Post Theme: Handing off from AI to me: what 20% actually means
Hook / Opening Line Idea: Claude Code got me to a real, working, tested MVP. Today I start the part only I can do.
Key Points to Include: Be honest about what 'AI did 80%' means in practice (schema design + implementation + testing, guided by your decisions at every step) vs what's left (product taste, real-world polish, your own judgment calls going forward)

Notes: Sets up the next phase of the series — good pivot post
